_schema._comment: SCHEMA DOCUMENTATION — read before editing. This file is the CANONICAL SOURCE of state tax data for Paths and Proofs. The inline ST={...} table in roth-conversion/index.html is GENERATED from this file by _dev/gen-st-table.mjs (run `npm run gen` after editing). NEVER hand-edit the inline ST table — edit states.json and regenerate. `npm run verify` runs a drift gate (gen-st-table.mjs --check) that fails if the committed HTML table does not match what this file would generate, plus bracket-sanity and cross-layer checks (check-states-json.mjs). The build-time inlining keeps the page fully self-contained (no runtime fetch — nothing leaves the user device) while making JSON-vs-HTML drift structurally impossible.

layering.facts: STATUTORY rate structure and tax treatment, INDEPENDENT of any tool. facts.brackets is the actual income-tax bracket table the state levies. This answers "what is this state's income tax?" — true regardless of whether you are modeling a conversion, an RMD, wages, or relocation. Future tools should read facts and apply their OWN interpretation.
layering.roth: CONVERSION-SPECIFIC interpretation for the Roth conversion tool. roth.cr = representative marginal rate used in the calc; roth.ex = whether a Roth conversion is exempt from state tax; roth.note = user-facing copy. This layer is guard-locked byte-for-byte to the inline ST table in index.html.
layering.importantWarning: facts and roth can LEGITIMATELY DIFFER and may look contradictory. Example: Illinois facts.brackets shows 4.95% (its real flat rate) while roth.ex=true (a conversion is exempt). Both are correct — they answer different questions. Do NOT "fix" an apparent contradiction by zeroing a facts rate; that would corrupt the rate structure other tools depend on. For ex:true states, roth.cr is the rate that WOULD apply if the exemption did not (the engine zeroes it via the ex flag).

fields.facts.brackets: Array of {rate, upTo}. rate is a decimal (0.0495 = 4.95%). upTo is the upper income bound of that bracket; the final (open-ended) bracket uses upTo:null. Thresholds strictly ascending. No-income-tax states use a single {rate:0, upTo:null}.
fields.facts.ssTreatment: Sourced prose on Social Security and retirement-income treatment, including the IRA-include-vs-exclude distinction where relevant.
fields.facts.incomeTax: Boolean; false for the nine no-income-tax states.
fields.facts.source: Primary-source URL (state Department of Revenue preferred).

facts.name: Alabama
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security and defined-benefit pensions exempt, but traditional IRA/401(k) distributions FULLY TAXABLE (a conversion is IRA income); $6,000 exclusion at 65+. Federal income tax is deductible from AL taxable income, lowering effective rate.

roth.note: Alabama exempts defined-benefit PENSIONS (government, military, many private) and Social Security — but traditional IRA and 401(k) distributions are FULLY TAXABLE at up to 5%. A Roth conversion is IRA income, so Alabama taxes it; those 65+ can exclude only the first $6,000. The tool treats the conversion as taxable, which is correct here.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: state 4%; ~9.3% combined (among highest); groceries taxed at reduced 3%

facts.name: Alaska
facts.ssTreatment: No state income tax; Social Security and all retirement income untaxed.

roth.note: No state income tax.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: NO state sales tax (some localities levy up to ~7%); no income tax

facts.name: Arizona
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security exempt. Flat 2.5% on IRA/conversion income.

roth.note: Flat 2.5% (one of the lowest in the nation), applied to IRA and conversion income. Social Security is exempt. The flat rate means a conversion is taxed at 2.5% regardless of size, making Arizona one of the cheaper states for a large conversion.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: state 5.6%; ~8.4% combined; groceries exempt at state level

facts.name: Arkansas
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security exempt below $100k federal AGI (taxable above, modified federal method). $6,000 retirement exemption covers traditional IRA but only at 59.5+; early distributions excluded. Net-income two-table system: filers over ~$94,700 use a compressed schedule.

roth.note: Top rate cut to 3.7% (2026 special session, S1), reached at low income (~$26,400), so a conversion is effectively taxed near 3.7%. A $6,000 retirement exemption covers traditional IRA income but only at 59.5+ (early distributions do not qualify), and it is a small cap a conversion exceeds. Social Security is exempt below $100k AGI; a large conversion could push past that.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: groceries taxed at reduced 0.125%; high local add-ons (~9.5% combined)

facts.name: California
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security exempt. All other retirement income — IRA, 401(k), pension, and Roth conversions — taxed as ordinary income (no preferential rate, no retirement exclusion). Top 13.3% includes the 1% Behavioral Health surcharge over $1M; thresholds inflation-indexed annually by FTB.
facts.bracketsNote: Single-filer thresholds, current FTB schedule; inflation-indexed yearly. MFJ thresholds ~2x except the $1M surcharge (not doubled).

roth.note: No retirement income exemption — IRA and Roth-conversion income is taxed as regular income through nine progressive brackets (1% to 12.3%), plus a 1% surcharge on income over $1M (13.3% top). The 9.3% bracket used here covers roughly $73k-$371k single income, where most filers land; your rate may be lower or higher. Social Security is not taxed by California.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: highest state base rate 7.25%; local add-ons to ~10.75%; groceries, prescriptions exempt

estateTax.note: No estate or inheritance tax (Prop 13 caps property assessment growth).

facts.name: Colorado
facts.ssTreatment: Fully exempts SS at 65+; on the SS-taxing list below that age. Flat 4.4% (TABOR may reduce). Pension/annuity subtraction covers IRA but excludes pre-59.5 distributions.

roth.note: Flat 4.4% (sometimes reduced by TABOR refunds). A pension/annuity subtraction covers IRA and conversion income — up to $20k at 55-64 and $24k at 65+ — but it specifically EXCLUDES early (pre-59.5) distributions, and a meaningful conversion exceeds the cap. Colorado fully exempts Social Security at 65+.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: low 2.9% state but high local add-ons (~7.8% combined)

facts.name: Connecticut
facts.ssTreatment: On the SS-taxing list: SS exempt below $75k single / $100k joint AGI, partial above. IRA/pension exemption reaches 100% in 2026 but ONLY under an AGI cliff ($75k/$100k full, phasing to zero at $100k/$150k) — a conversion raises AGI and a sizable one blows past the cliff, making it taxable (NJ-style). Military/railroad retirement fully exempt. Tax-recapture provision can lift effective rates for high earners.
facts.bracketsNote: Single-filer thresholds; MFJ ~2x. Source: CT OLR 2026-R-0057 + DRS CT-1040 instructions.

roth.note: For 2026 Connecticut fully exempts IRA, 401(k), and pension income ONLY if AGI stays under $75,000 single / $100,000 joint, phasing out to nothing at a $100,000 / $150,000 cliff. A Roth conversion raises AGI and a sizable one can blow past the cliff, making it taxable (graduated 2% to 6.99%; about 5.5% is typical). This tool assumes the conversion is taxable; a small one that keeps you under the threshold may not be. Connecticut also taxes Social Security above those same thresholds.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: groceries, prescriptions exempt; no local add-on

facts.name: Delaware
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security exempt; no sales tax. At 60+, a $12,500 exclusion covers combined pension + eligible retirement income (includes IRA), but it is a small cap a meaningful conversion exceeds, and early (pre-59.5) distributions do not qualify. The 6.6% top applies above $60k; a typical conversion lands in the 5.55% band.
facts.bracketsNote: Confirmed against DE Division of Revenue official rate table (software-developer page). Same thresholds for all filing statuses. NOTE: HB13 proposed new brackets above $125k (6.75%+) but is NOT enacted for 2026 — the DE DOR table still shows 6.6% top; several secondary sources wrongly reported the proposal as law. Revisit if HB13 passes.

roth.note: Graduated 2.2% to 6.6%; the 6.6% top starts at just $60k, so a conversion that brings total income near $100k lands at the 6.6% used here (totals under $60k pay 5.55% or less). At 60+, a $12,500 exclusion covers combined pension + IRA income, but it is a small cap a meaningful conversion exceeds — and an early distribution (before 59.5) does not qualify at all. Social Security is exempt and there is no sales tax.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: NO sales tax

facts.name: Florida
facts.ssTreatment: No state income tax; constitutionally prohibited. SS and retirement income untaxed.

roth.note: No state income tax.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: groceries, prescriptions exempt; counties add up to 2%

estateTax.note: No estate or inheritance tax; no state income tax.

facts.name: Georgia
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security fully exempt. Flat 4.99% (HB 463, signed May 2026, down from 5.39%). Retirement income exclusion is a CAP — $65,000/person at 65+ ($35,000 at 62-64) — covering pension/IRA/401k; a conversion exceeding the cap is taxable on the excess.
facts.bracketsNote: Flat tax (no brackets) since 2024 HB 1437 transition; HB 463 accelerated to 4.99% for 2026. Verified vs GA DOR.

roth.note: Georgia excludes retirement income up to a CAP — $65,000 per person at 65+ ($35,000 at 62-64) — then taxes the rest at its flat 4.99% rate (cut from 5.39% under HB 463, signed May 2026). A Roth conversion is large income that can exceed the cap, so the excess is taxable; this tool assumes the conversion is taxable, though a small conversion that fits under your exclusion may not be. Social Security is fully exempt and does not use up the cap.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: state 4%; ~7.4% combined; groceries exempt at state level

facts.name: Hawaii
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security and employer-funded PENSIONS fully exempt (HRS 235-7(a)(3)), but traditional IRA/401(k) income is fully taxable — a conversion is IRA income, so Hawaii taxes it. A pension-holder should not assume the conversion is also exempt.
facts.bracketsNote: Single-filer, Act 46 current schedule, exact thresholds from Tax Foundation of Hawaii (HRS Ch.235): 12 brackets 1.4%-11%, 11% top over $200k. Act 46 widens thresholds in stages through 2031, so upper edges shift in TY2027/2029. MFJ thresholds ~2x.

roth.note: Graduated 1.4% to 11% across twelve brackets; the 11% top only applies above $200k single / $400k joint, and Act 46 (2024) is widening the brackets in stages through 2031. A typical conversion lands in the 8.25% band ($48k-$150k single, used here). Hawaii fully exempts pensions and Social Security, but traditional IRA and 401(k) income is fully taxable — a Roth conversion is IRA income, so Hawaii taxes it.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: General Excise Tax 4-4.5% (taxes services, applied broadly); groceries taxed

facts.name: Idaho
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security exempt. Flat 5.3% (HB40 2025, retroactive) on IRA/conversion income.

roth.note: Flat 5.3% (cut from 5.695% under 2025 reform, retroactive to that year), applied to IRA and conversion income. Social Security is exempt. Being flat, the rate on a conversion does not change with its size.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: groceries taxed (with a credit); local resort taxes in some towns

facts.name: Illinois
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security exempt. Flat 4.95%, but qualified retirement income — including the taxable portion of a Roth conversion — is exempt via Schedule M with no age/income/cliff condition (roth.ex=true).

roth.note: Illinois exempts qualified retirement income — IRA, 401(k), and pension — from its 4.95% flat tax, and the Department of Revenue treats the taxable portion of a Roth conversion as exempt too (subtracted on Schedule M). No age requirement, no income cap, no cliff — so a conversion is genuinely Illinois-tax-free. Social Security is also exempt.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: state 6.25%; ~8.85% combined; groceries 1% (being eliminated)

facts.name: Indiana
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security exempt. Flat 2.95% for 2026 (falling toward ~2.9% in 2027); some counties add local income tax.

roth.note: Flat 2.95% for 2026 (cut from 3.05%, scheduled to keep falling toward ~2.9% in 2027), applied to IRA and conversion income. Social Security is exempt. The flat rate means a conversion is taxed the same regardless of size; some counties add a small local income tax on top.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: flat 7% state, no local add-on; groceries exempt

facts.name: Iowa
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security fully exempt at any age. Since 2023, ALL retirement income — pensions, IRA, 401(k), including Roth conversion income — is fully exempt for residents age 55+ (no dollar cap), so a conversion at 55+ is Iowa-tax-free (roth.ex=true). Under 55, taxed at the flat 3.8% rate. Iowa Code 422.5.
facts.bracketsNote: Flat 3.8% for 2025-2026 (SF 2442, May 2024, collapsed the old graduated brackets; rate fell 8.53%/2022 -> 6%/2023 -> 4.82%/2024 -> 3.8%/2025). Verified vs Iowa DOR / LegalClarity citing IDR.

roth.note: Since 2023 Iowa fully exempts retirement income — including Roth conversion income — for residents age 55 and older (no dollar cap), so a conversion at 55+ is Iowa-tax-free. Under 55, the conversion is taxable at Iowa's flat 3.8% rate (SF 2442 collapsed the old brackets to a single flat rate for 2025+). Social Security is fully exempt at any age.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: state 6%; ~6.9% combined; groceries exempt

facts.name: Kansas
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security FULLY exempt regardless of income (SB 1, TY2024+ — the old $75k threshold is repealed). KPERS, federal, military, and railroad pensions fully exempt; but PRIVATE IRA/401(k) distributions are fully taxable — a conversion is private IRA income, so Kansas taxes it.
facts.bracketsNote: Two-bracket SB 1 (2024 Special Session) schedule, confirmed against KS DOR Legislative Changes doc citing K.S.A. 79-32,110(a): 5.2% to $23k, 5.58% above (single; MFJ $46k). A secondary source erroneously cited 3.1%/5.7% — that is wrong per the DOR statute citation.

roth.note: Two brackets; the top ~5.58% rate starts at just ~$23k single, so a typical conversion is taxed near that rate. IRA, 401(k), and conversion income is fully taxable (KPERS, federal, and military pensions are exempt, but private retirement accounts are not). Kansas fully exempted Social Security starting in 2024, so a conversion here does not pull benefits into tax.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: state 6.5% (state grocery tax repealed 2025); ~8.7% combined

facts.name: Kentucky
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security exempt. Flat 3.5% (cut from 4% Jan 1 2026 via revenue trigger); modest retirement exclusion.

roth.note: Flat 3.5% (cut from 4% on Jan 1 2026 via a revenue trigger), applied to IRA and conversion income. Kentucky exempts Social Security and gives a modest retirement exclusion, but a meaningful conversion is taxable beyond it.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: flat 6% state, no local add-on; groceries exempt

estateTax.note: Inheritance tax 0% for immediate family (Class A); 4-16% for others.

facts.name: Louisiana
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security fully exempt; most LA government, federal, railroad, and military pensions also exempt. Private IRA/401(k)/conversion income taxable, but a $12,000-per-person retirement-income exclusion at 65+ (doubled from $6k under Act 11, inflation-adjusted from 2026) shelters part of it. Louisiana also lets you deduct federal income tax paid, lowering the effective rate.
facts.bracketsNote: Flat 3% (Act 11, 2024 3rd Extraordinary Session, effective Jan 1 2025; replaced the old 1.85%-4.25% graduated structure). Confirmed against LA DOR FAQ. Revenue-trigger mechanism may cut the rate further from 2026.

roth.note: Flat 3% (2025 reform, Act 11/HB1 — replaced the old graduated structure; LA DOR confirms). IRA and conversion income is taxable, with a $12,000 retirement-income exclusion per person at 65+ (doubled from $6k, inflation-adjusted). A conversion above that is taxable at 3%. Social Security and most LA government/military pensions are fully exempt.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: highest combined sales tax in US (~9.5%) with local add-ons; groceries exempt at state level

facts.name: Maine
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security fully exempt. Maine has a pension income deduction (up to ~$48k, AGI-phaseout over $125k single / $250k joint) — BUT it specifically EXCLUDES Roth conversions (along with 457(f) and early-penalty distributions). So a Roth conversion gets NO shelter and is fully taxable. The old tool note wrongly implied the deduction applied; this is the IRA-exclusion-trap family (cf. RI, MD).
facts.bracketsNote: Single-filer, Maine Revenue Services official 2026 schedule (published 09/15/2025): 5.8% <$27,400, 6.75% to $64,850, 7.15% above. MFJ thresholds ~2x. Inflation-indexed annually. Conversion-exclusion from the pension deduction confirmed via Maine guidance during audit.

roth.note: Graduated 5.8% to 7.15%; the 7.15% top starts near $65k single / $130k joint, so a conversion that brings total income near $100k lands at the 7.15% used here (smaller totals pay 6.75% or less). Important: Maine's pension income deduction does NOT apply to Roth conversions — they are specifically excluded — so the conversion is fully taxable with no shelter. Social Security is exempt.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: groceries, prescriptions exempt; no local add-on

facts.name: Maryland
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security fully exempt. The pension exclusion (~$36k-$41k at 65+) covers QUALIFIED employer-plan/pension income but EXCLUDES traditional IRA and 401(k) distributions — a Roth conversion is IRA income, so it gets NO shelter and is fully taxable. (Rolling a 401k into an IRA even forfeits exclusion eligibility.) IRA-exclusion-trap family (cf. ME, RI). Note: every county adds a mandatory 2.25%-3.30% local tax on top of the state rate.
facts.bracketsNote: Single-filer; 4.75% band (k-$100k) is where most retirement income lands; 5.75% only over $250k. NEW 2026 brackets (H.B. 352 / FY26 budget): 6.25% over $500k, 6.5% over $1M. roth.cr kept at 5.75% (state portion; county tax 2.25-3.3% adds on top, roughly offsetting). Plus 2% capital-gains surtax over $350k FAGI. Source: MD Comptroller 2026 withholding.

roth.note: State 2% to 5.75% (plus new 6.25% and 6.5% brackets above ~$250k), AND a mandatory county tax of about 2.25% to 3.2% on top — so the real rate is higher than the state figure shown. Important: Maryland's pension exclusion (about $36k at 65+) covers 401(k)/403(b)/pension income but EXCLUDES all IRAs, so a Roth conversion gets no shelter and is fully taxable. Social Security is exempt.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: groceries, prescriptions exempt

estateTax.note: Estate tax ($5M exemption) AND inheritance tax on non-lineal heirs. Plus mandatory county income tax 2.25-3.30% (Tier-2: not modeled).

facts.name: Massachusetts
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security exempt. Flat 5% with NO retirement exclusion (IRA/401k fully taxed); 4% surtax stacks above $1M income (9% effective on the excess) — a very large conversion can trip it.

roth.note: Flat 5% on most income including IRA and conversion income, with NO retirement exclusion (Massachusetts taxes IRA/401(k) withdrawals in full). Watch the 4% surtax on income above $1M: a very large conversion could push that year past the threshold and add 4% on the excess. Social Security is exempt.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: 6.25% no local add-on; groceries, clothing under $175 exempt

facts.name: Michigan
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security exempt. Flat 4.25%; PA4 retirement deduction phase-in complete 2026 (deduct ~$67,610 single / $135,220 joint of retirement income).

roth.note: Flat 4.25%. For 2026 the Lowering MI Costs phase-in is complete: retirees can deduct retirement and pension income (including IRA withdrawals) up to about $67,610 single / $135,220 joint, regardless of birth year. Social Security is fully exempt from Michigan tax. A Roth conversion is taxable IRA income, so whether it falls under the deduction cap depends on your other retirement income — verify for your situation.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: flat 6% state, no local add-on; groceries exempt

facts.name: Minnesota
facts.ssTreatment: On the SS-taxing list: SS fully exempt below $82,190 single / $105,380 joint AGI (2026), phasing out above. NO retirement-income exclusion — pension, IRA, 401(k), and Roth conversion income all taxed as ordinary income at full rates. A conversion both adds taxable income and can push past the SS-subtraction threshold, pulling more benefits into tax.
facts.bracketsNote: Single-filer, MN DOR official 2026 schedule (inflation-adjusted, via kare11/MN Revenue): 9.85% top only over $203,150. MFJ thresholds wider. roth.cr 7.85% representative (top only above $203k).

roth.note: Graduated 5.35% to 9.85%; the 6.8% band runs to about $109k single, so a conversion that brings total income near $100k lands at the 6.8% used here (7.85% applies from ~$109k to ~$203k, 9.85% above). IRA and Roth-conversion income is fully taxable (no broad retirement exclusion). Watch a second effect: a conversion raises your income and can shrink Minnesota's Social Security subtraction, pulling more of your benefits into MN tax that year.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: groceries, clothing, prescriptions exempt

facts.name: Mississippi
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security fully exempt. Mississippi exempts QUALIFIED retirement income (IRA, 401k, pension) once retirement requirements met (generally 59.5) — so a conversion at 59.5+ is MS-tax-free (roth.ex=true). An EARLY distribution before 59.5 does NOT qualify and is taxed at the flat rate.
facts.bracketsNote: Flat 4.4% above a $10k 0%-bracket for 2026 (stepping down toward 3.75% in coming years under the 2022 phasedown). roth.cr 4.4% is the rate that would apply pre-exemption.

roth.note: Mississippi exempts qualified retirement income — IRA, 401(k), and pension — once you have met the plan's retirement requirements (generally age 59.5). A conversion at 59.5+ is therefore MS-tax-free; an EARLY distribution before then does not qualify and is taxed at the flat rate (4.4% for 2026, stepping down toward 3.75%). Social Security is fully exempt.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: state 7% (groceries taxed at full 7%, among few that do); ~7.1% combined

facts.name: Missouri
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security fully exempt (62+). Private IRA/401(k)/conversion income taxable; only break is a $6,000 private-pension exclusion that phases out under $25k single / $32k joint MAGI, so a meaningful conversion rarely qualifies. Public pensions get a larger exclusion (up to ~$48,967) but that does not cover private IRA income. Capital gains now fully exempt (2025+).
facts.bracketsNote: Single-filer (same thresholds all statuses); top 4.7% starts ~$9,191. Top rate cut 4.8%->4.7% Jan 2025 (SB 3 revenue trigger). Inflation-indexed. Source: Mo. Rev. Stat. 143.011 via MO DOR.

roth.note: Graduated 2% to 4.7%, but the 4.7% top rate starts at just ~$9,200 of taxable income, so a conversion is effectively taxed at 4.7%. IRA and conversion income is taxable; the only applicable break is a $6,000 private-pension exclusion that phases out under $25k single / $32k joint, so a meaningful conversion rarely qualifies. Social Security is fully exempt (62+).

taxContext.salesTaxNote: state 4.225%; ~8.4% combined; groceries 1.225%

facts.name: Montana
facts.ssTreatment: On the SS-taxing list: Montana taxes Social Security following the federal formula, so a conversion that raises income pulls more benefits into tax. IRA/conversion income taxable; Montana REPEALED its broader pension/IRA deduction for 2025, leaving only a small (~$5,500) qualified-retirement-income deduction. No sales tax.
facts.bracketsNote: Single-filer two-bracket HB337 schedule: 4.7% to ~$20,500, 5.65% above (5.65% for 2026, falling to 5.4% in 2027). MFJ second bracket starts ~$41,000. Source: Tax Foundation 2026 / MT DOR HB337.

roth.note: Two brackets (4.7% / 5.65% for 2026, top rate falling to 5.4% in 2027); the 5.65% rate starts at just ~$20,500 single / ~$41,000 joint, so most conversion income is taxed at 5.65%. IRA and conversion income is taxable; Montana repealed its broader retirement deduction and now allows only a small (~$5,500) qualified-retirement-income deduction. Montana also taxes Social Security on the federal formula, so a conversion can pull more of your benefits into tax. No sales tax.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: NO general sales tax (some resort-town local taxes)

facts.name: Nebraska
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security FULLY exempt (2025+) regardless of income — so unlike some neighbors, a conversion here does not pull more SS into tax. IRA/conversion income fully taxable with no special retirement exclusion.
facts.bracketsNote: Single-filer 2026 three-bracket schedule (LB 754 collapsed the prior four brackets — the 2025 5.01% and 5.20% rates BOTH drop to 4.55% for 2026, merging into the top bracket). Top 4.55% starts at $18,000 single. Falls to 3.99% in 2027. Thresholds NOT inflation-indexed. Source: Neb. Rev. Stat. 77-2715.03 / NE DOR.

roth.note: Top rate is 4.55% for 2026 (down from 5.84%; falling to 3.99% in 2027 under LB 754), and a typical conversion lands at or near that top rate. IRA and conversion income is fully taxable with no special exemption. Social Security is now fully exempt (as of 2025) regardless of income — so unlike some neighbors, a conversion here does not pull more SS into tax.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: state 5.5%; ~6.97% combined; groceries exempt

estateTax.note: County inheritance tax: 1% lineal (over $100k), higher for others. No estate tax.

facts.name: Nevada
facts.ssTreatment: No state income tax; constitutionally entrenched. SS and retirement income untaxed.

roth.note: No state income tax.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: state 6.85%; ~8.2% combined w/ local; groceries, prescriptions exempt

facts.name: New Hampshire
facts.ssTreatment: No tax on wages or retirement income; Interest & Dividends Tax repealed effective Jan 1 2025 (never applied to IRA income).

roth.note: No income tax (interest/dividend tax ended 2025).

taxContext.salesTaxNote: NO general sales tax (8.5% meals & rooms tax); Interest & Dividends tax REPEALED Jan 1 2025 (never applied to IRA income)

facts.name: New Jersey
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security fully exempt at all income levels. NJ has NO broad retirement exclusion like IL/PA. The pension/IRA exclusion (up to $75k single / $100k joint of retirement income) applies for those 62+ ONLY if total income stays at or below a HARD $150k cliff — over $150k, the entire exclusion vanishes. A Roth conversion counts toward that income, so a sizable one blows past the cliff and makes the whole conversion taxable (rates to 10.75%). Note: NJ does not allow a deduction for traditional IRA contributions, so part of a distribution may be NJ basis already taxed.
facts.bracketsNote: Single-filer; the 6.37% band (5k-$500k) is where most conversion income lands. Statutory (not inflation-indexed). 8.97% over $500k, 10.75% over $1M. Source: NJ Division of Taxation.

roth.note: New Jersey fully excludes retirement income (including IRA withdrawals) for those 62+ ONLY if total income stays at or below $100,000, phasing out to nothing at a hard $150,000 cliff. A Roth conversion counts toward that income, so a sizable one can push you over the cliff and make the whole conversion taxable at rates up to 10.75% (6.37% is typical). This tool assumes the conversion is taxable; if your total income stays under the threshold it may not be. Social Security is fully exempt.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: groceries, clothing, prescriptions exempt

estateTax.note: Estate tax repealed 2018; inheritance tax still applies to non-lineal heirs.

facts.name: New Mexico
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security exempt for AGI under $100k single / $150k joint (2023 reform); ABOVE those thresholds SS is taxed at full rates. IRA/conversion income taxable; those 65+ may deduct up to $8,000 of retirement income but only under income limits. A conversion raises AGI and can blow past BOTH the $8k-deduction limit AND the $100k/$150k SS-exemption threshold — a double cliff. Military retirement fully exempt. No traditional sales tax (Gross Receipts Tax instead).
facts.bracketsNote: Single-filer, 6-bracket HB252 structure (added the 4.3% bracket, restructured thresholds). 5.9% top only over $210k single / $315k joint. roth.cr 4.9% representative. Source: NM Taxation & Revenue Dept.

roth.note: Graduated 1.5% to 5.9%; the 5.9% top only applies above $210k single / $315k joint, so a typical conversion lands near the 4.9% band used here. IRA and conversion income is taxable; those 65+ can deduct up to $8,000, but only under $100k single / $150k joint AGI. A conversion raises AGI and can blow past that limit — and past the same $100k/$150k threshold above which New Mexico starts taxing Social Security too.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: Gross Receipts Tax ~5% state (taxes services too); ~7.9-8.4% combined

facts.name: New York
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security and all government pensions (federal/state/local) fully exempt. Private IRA/401(k)/conversion income taxable, but those 59.5+ can exclude $20,000 of private retirement income per person (still $20k for 2026 — proposed increases NOT enacted). A conversion above $20k is taxable. NYC residents add 3.078%-3.876% city tax; Yonkers adds a surcharge.
facts.bracketsNote: Single-filer. The 2026 state budget CUT rates in the lower five brackets (income under ~$215,400); aggregators report a mix of pre- and post-cut figures. These reflect the post-cut ladder (NerdWallet/Wealthvieu): 4/4.5/5.25/5.5/6/6.85/9.65/10.3/10.9. The old 6.25% mid-bracket was replaced — roth.cr refreshed 6.25%->6% to match the current band a typical conversion lands in. High brackets (6.85%+) unchanged, extended through 2032.

roth.note: Graduated 4% to 10.9% (the 10.9% top only applies above $25M; 6% here reflects a typical conversion landing in the ~$80k-$215k band, after the 2026 budget cut the mid-brackets). IRA and Roth-conversion income is taxable, but those 59.5+ can exclude $20,000 of private retirement income (per person; still $20,000 for 2026 — proposed increases were not enacted). Government pensions and Social Security are fully exempt. NYC residents also pay city tax up to ~3.876%.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: state 4%; local add-ons to ~8.5% (NYC 8.875%); groceries, prescriptions exempt. NYC/Yonkers residents owe LOCAL income tax (Tier-2: not modeled)

estateTax.note: ~$7.16M exemption with a notorious cliff (estates ~5% over exemption lose the WHOLE exemption).

facts.name: North Carolina
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security exempt. Flat 3.99% for 2026 (phasedown continuing toward 2.99% by 2028).

roth.note: Flat 3.99% for 2026 (final step of a phasedown; scheduled to keep falling toward 2.99% by 2028), applied to IRA and conversion income. Social Security is exempt. Being flat, the conversion is taxed at the same rate regardless of size.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: state 4.75%; ~7% combined; groceries exempt at state level

facts.name: North Dakota
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security fully exempt. IRA/conversion income taxable but at among the lowest rates in the nation (top 2.5%). The 0% bottom bracket (up to ~$47k single taxable income) means part of a modest conversion may be untaxed entirely.
facts.bracketsNote: Single-filer; ND has a 0% bottom bracket then 1.95% and 2.5% (top 2.5% over ~$244,825 single). Two taxable brackets above the zero bracket. Source: ND OST / TurboTax 2026. roth.cr 2.5% is the top (conservative for a sizable conversion).

roth.note: Very low rates topping out at 2.5%, applied to IRA and conversion income — among the lowest state taxes on a conversion anywhere. The 2.5% top starts near $245k, so a conversion that brings total income near $100k lands at the 1.95% used here. Social Security is exempt. North Dakota has a 0% bottom bracket, so part of a small conversion may not be taxed at all.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: state 5%; ~7% combined; groceries exempt

facts.name: Ohio
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security fully exempt. Military retirement exempt. Private IRA/401(k)/conversion income taxable at the flat 2.75%; a small retirement-income credit (up to ~$200 total) barely offsets a conversion. Some Ohio municipalities levy local income tax, but it generally does not apply to retirement-plan distributions.
facts.bracketsNote: HB96 moved Ohio to a FLAT 2.75% on nonbusiness income over $26,050 for 2026 (was two-bracket 2.75%/3.125% in 2025). 0% below $26,050. roth.cr 2.75% confirmed current. Source: Tax Foundation 2026 / OH Dept of Taxation.

roth.note: For 2026 Ohio uses a flat 2.75% on income above $26,050 (0% below). IRA and Roth-conversion income is taxable at that rate; a small retirement-income credit (up to $200 total) barely offsets a conversion. Social Security is fully exempt from Ohio tax.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: state 5.75%; ~7.24% combined; groceries exempt

facts.name: Oklahoma
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security fully exempt. Private IRA/401(k)/conversion income taxable; a $10,000 retirement-income exclusion (65+) DOES include IRA income but is a small cap a meaningful conversion exceeds. Military retirees may exclude the greater of $10k or 75%.
facts.bracketsNote: HB2764 (signed May 2025) collapsed six brackets into a 0% bottom plus three taxable rates (2.5%/3.5%/4.5%) for 2026, top cut 4.75%->4.5%. Single-filer thresholds; not inflation-indexed. Revenue-trigger may cut further. roth.cr 4.5% confirmed current. Source: OK Tax Commission 2026 withholding tables.

roth.note: Oklahoma taxes traditional IRA and 401(k) conversion income; the top rate for tax year 2025 (the latest year with a complete published bracket table) is 4.75%, reached at low income, so a conversion is effectively taxed at 4.75%. A 2025 law, House Bill 2764, cuts the top rate to 4.5% and moves to three brackets beginning tax year 2026, but the full 2026 bracket amounts are not yet published, so this tool uses the confirmed 2025 schedule. The $10,000 retirement-income exclusion (age 65+) does include IRA income, but it is a small cap a meaningful conversion exceeds, leaving the rest taxable. Social Security is fully exempt.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: groceries: state grocery tax repealed 2024; local add-ons remain (~8.9% combined)

facts.name: Oregon
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security fully exempt. Nearly all other retirement income — IRA, 401(k), pension, and Roth conversions — taxed as ordinary income at full rates; the retirement-income credit is limited to low household income and rarely helps a conversion. Oregon allows a federal-tax-paid subtraction (up to ~$7,050 single) that modestly lowers the effective rate. No sales tax. Portland-metro residents owe additional local income taxes (SHS/Metro/PFA).
facts.bracketsNote: Single-filer, OR DOR 2026 schedule: 8.75% band runs $10,200-$125,000 (where most conversion income lands), 9.9% above $125k. Oregon does not fully index top brackets. MFJ thresholds differ. roth.cr 8.75% representative.

roth.note: Graduated 4.75% to 9.9%; the 8.75% bracket runs up to $125,000, so most conversion income lands there (9.9% applies above that). IRA and Roth-conversion income is fully taxable, and the retirement-income credit is limited to low household income, so it rarely helps with a conversion. Social Security is fully exempt; Oregon has no sales tax. Portland-area residents may owe extra county/metro income tax.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: NO state or local sales tax

facts.name: Pennsylvania
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security exempt. Flat 3.07%, but retirement income is exempt once at retirement age (59.5 for IRAs), so a conversion at 59.5+ is PA-tax-free (roth.ex=true); an early distribution could be taxed.

roth.note: Pennsylvania exempts retirement income — IRA, 401(k), and pension distributions — from its flat 3.07% tax once you reach retirement age (59.5 for IRAs). A Roth conversion at 59.5+ is therefore PA-tax-free; a conversion before 59.5 could be taxed as an early distribution. Social Security is fully exempt. (PA also taxes 401(k)/IRA contributions up front, so there is no further state tax at withdrawal.)

taxContext.salesTaxNote: groceries, clothing, prescriptions exempt; some localities (Philadelphia, Allegheny) add 1-2% (Tier-2: not modeled)

estateTax.note: Inheritance tax 0% spouse / 4.5% lineal / 12-15% others; no exemption floor for non-spouse.

facts.name: Rhode Island
facts.ssTreatment: On the SS-taxing list: SS exempt below ~$107k single / ~$133.75k joint federal AGI (2026), taxed above. Rhode Island has a retirement-income modification (up to $50,000 per person at Full Retirement Age for 2026, up from $20k) — but it covers ONLY 401(k)/403(b)/annuity/pension income and EXPLICITLY EXCLUDES all IRAs (traditional, Roth, SEP, SIMPLE). A Roth conversion is IRA income, so it gets NO shelter and is fully taxable. IRA-exclusion-trap family (cf. ME, MD) — RI is the most explicit, naming Roth. Hard all-or-nothing income cliff: $1 over the limit forfeits the entire exclusion. Military pensions fully exempt.
facts.bracketsNote: Same thresholds all filing statuses (no MFJ doubling); inflation-indexed. 5.99% top over $186,450. roth.cr 4.75% representative (typical conversion lands in middle band). Source: RI Division of Taxation 2026.

roth.note: Three brackets (3.75% / 4.75% / 5.99%); 5.99% only starts near $176k, so a typical conversion lands in the 4.75% band used here. Important: Rhode Island's up-to-$50,000 retirement-income modification covers 401(k)/403(b)/pension/annuity income but specifically EXCLUDES all IRAs — so a Roth conversion gets no shelter and is fully taxable. Rhode Island also taxes Social Security above ~$107k single / ~$133.75k joint, a threshold a conversion can cross.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: groceries, clothing under $250 exempt; no local add-on

facts.name: South Carolina
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security fully exempt at all ages/incomes. Military retirement fully exempt. The retirement-income deduction ($3,000 under 65 / $10,000 at 65+) DOES cover traditional IRA income; PLUS a separate $15,000 age-65 deduction against ANY income — so post-65 is a favorable conversion window. A qualified Roth distribution is already federally excluded and carries over to SC, but a Roth CONVERSION is taxable income (reduced by available deductions).
facts.bracketsNote: H.4216 (signed Mar 30 2026) created a two-bracket 2026 structure: 1.99% to $30k, 5.21% above (SC DOR via LegalClarity). NOTE: sources conflict heavily — older/transitional sources cite 0%/3%/6%, a flat 5%, or 6%/6.2%; the Tax Foundation noted a temp 6% reverting to 6.2% before H.4216 superseded it. The 1.99%/5.21% reflects the newest enacted law. roth.cr refreshed 5.2%->5.21% to match the top bracket. Revenue-triggered cuts begin 2027.

roth.note: Two-bracket system for 2026 under H.4216 (signed March 2026): 1.99% on the first $30,000, then 5.21% above. The retirement deduction DOES cover traditional IRA income; those 65+ get a $15,000 deduction against any income that can absorb conversion income — so post-65 is a relatively favorable conversion window here. Social Security is fully exempt. (Note: qualified Roth distributions are already federally excluded and carry over, but a Roth conversion is taxable income.)

taxContext.salesTaxNote: groceries exempt; counties add up to 3%

facts.name: South Dakota
facts.ssTreatment: No state income tax. SS and retirement income untaxed.

roth.note: No state income tax.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: state 4.2%; ~6.1% combined; groceries taxed

facts.name: Tennessee
facts.ssTreatment: No income tax; Hall tax on interest/dividends repealed Jan 1 2021; constitutionally protected (Amendment 3, 2014). SS and retirement income untaxed.

roth.note: No income tax.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: state 7% (highest base); ~9.55% combined; groceries taxed at reduced 4%

facts.name: Texas
facts.ssTreatment: No state income tax; constitutionally prohibited. SS and retirement income untaxed.

roth.note: No state income tax.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: state 6.25%; ~8.2% combined; groceries, prescriptions exempt

facts.name: Utah
facts.ssTreatment: On the SS-taxing list (taxes SS with a phased credit for lower incomes). Flat 4.5% (recently cut); small income-phased retirement credit.

roth.note: Flat 4.5% (recently cut), applied to IRA and conversion income. Utah is one of the few states that taxes Social Security, though a phased credit offsets it for lower incomes; the flat structure means a conversion is taxed at the same rate at any size. A small retirement credit applies but phases out as income rises.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: state 4.85% (1.75% on groceries); ~7.2% combined

facts.name: Vermont
facts.ssTreatment: On the SS-taxing list: SS exempt below income thresholds (~$50k single / $65k joint AGI), phasing out above. Private IRA/401(k)/conversion income fully taxable; only a small $10,000 deduction for certain government/pension retirement income (income-limited, excludes most IRA). A conversion can also push past the SS-exemption threshold, taxing more benefits that year. Military retirement deduction up to $10k.
facts.bracketsNote: Single-filer; 8.75% top near $242k. roth.cr 6.6% representative (typical conversion lands in the 6.6% band, $47,900-$116,000). Inflation-indexed. Source: VT Dept of Taxes.

roth.note: Graduated 3.35% to 8.75%; the 8.75% top only starts near $230k single / $280k joint, so a typical conversion lands in the 6.6% band used here. IRA and Roth-conversion income is fully taxable (only a small $10k deduction for certain pension/government retirement income, income-limited). A conversion can also push you past Vermont's Social Security exemption thresholds, taxing more of your benefits that year.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: 6% state; local option up to 1% (~6.39% combined); groceries, clothing exempt

facts.name: Virginia
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security fully exempt. Private IRA/401(k)/pension/conversion income taxed at full rates — and the top 5.75% rate starts at just $17,000, so essentially all conversion income is taxed at 5.75%. Those 65+ get a $12,000 age deduction, but it phases out dollar-for-dollar above $50k single / $75k joint AGI — a conversion raises AGI and a large one can erase the deduction (a cliff). Military retirement up to $40k exempt.
facts.bracketsNote: Single-filer; thresholds unchanged for decades, same for all filing statuses. Top 5.75% over $17,000 (effectively near-flat for most). Source: VA Dept of Taxation. NOTE: VA standard deduction is scheduled to revert sharply after TY2026 unless extended.

roth.note: Top rate 5.75% starts at just $17,000, so essentially all conversion income is taxed at 5.75%. IRA and Roth-conversion income is taxable. Those 65+ get a $12,000 age deduction, but it phases out dollar-for-dollar above $50,000 single / $75,000 joint AGI — and a conversion raises your AGI, so a large one can erase the deduction. Social Security is fully exempt.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: state 5.3%; ~5.77% combined; groceries 1%

facts.name: Washington
facts.ssTreatment: No income tax on ordinary income (7% capital-gains tax exempts retirement accounts). NOTE: ESSB 6346 adds a 9.9% tax on household income >$1M effective Jan 1 2028, counting conversion income; revisit before TY2028.

roth.note: No income tax, so IRA and Roth-conversion income is untaxed (the 7% capital-gains tax exempts retirement accounts). One thing to watch: a new state income tax of 9.9% on household income above $1 million takes effect in 2028 and counts conversion income toward that threshold — so only a very large conversion in 2028 or later could be affected. Social Security is exempt.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: state 6.5%; local add-ons push combined to ~9-10% (Tier-2: state rate only modeled)

estateTax.note: $2.193M exemption, 10-20% rates (one of the lowest exemption floors).

facts.name: West Virginia
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security FULLY exempt for all residents as of 2026 (phase-out completed). Private IRA/401(k)/conversion income taxable; seniors 65+ get an $8,000 modification. Military retirement fully exempt. West Virginia is cutting income tax most years via revenue triggers, so rates keep falling.
facts.bracketsNote: Single-filer 2026 schedule after SB 392 (2026 session, 5% across-the-board cut, top 4.82%->4.58% eff Jan 1 2026). Exact rates 2.11/2.81/3.16/4.22/4.58 per Thomson Reuters citing the enacted law. Many secondary sources still show the stale 5.12% (two cuts behind) or 4.82% (one behind). roth.cr refreshed to 4.58%. Source: WV State Tax Dept / SB 392.

roth.note: Top rate cut to 4.58% effective Jan 1 2026 (SB 392, the 2026 session 5% across-the-board cut; West Virginia is cutting income tax most years, so this keeps falling). IRA and Roth-conversion income is taxable; seniors 65+ get an $8,000 modification. Social Security is now fully exempt for all residents (phase-out completed in 2026).

taxContext.salesTaxNote: state 6%; ~6.57% combined; groceries exempt

facts.name: Wisconsin
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security fully exempt. Private IRA/401(k)/conversion income taxable, BUT 2025 Act 15 lets those 67+ subtract up to $24,000 ($48,000 joint) of retirement income INCLUDING IRA/conversion income — so post-67 part of a conversion may be sheltered (a relatively favorable window). Federal/state government pensions for pre-1964 hires fully exempt.
facts.bracketsNote: Single-filer; 7.65% top only over ~$323k, so a typical conversion lands in the broad 5.3% band ($29,370-$323,290). roth.cr 5.3% representative. Inflation-indexed. Source: WI Dept of Revenue.

roth.note: Graduated 3.5% to 7.65%; the 7.65% top only starts above ~$315k single / ~$420k joint, so a typical conversion lands in the 5.3% band used here. IRA and Roth-conversion income is taxable, but a 2025 law (Act 15) lets those 67+ subtract up to $24,000 ($48,000 joint) of retirement income — including IRA/conversion income — so part of a conversion may be sheltered. Social Security is fully exempt.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: groceries, prescriptions exempt; counties add ~0.5%

facts.name: Wyoming
facts.ssTreatment: No state income tax. SS and retirement income untaxed.

roth.note: No state income tax.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: state 4%; ~5.4% combined; groceries exempt

facts.name: Washington D.C.
facts.ssTreatment: Social Security exempt. All other retirement income — pension, IRA, 401(k), and Roth conversions — taxed at ordinary rates; no retirement-income exclusion. The 8.5% band covers the broad $60k-$250k range where most conversion income lands; 10.75% only above $1M.
facts.bracketsNote: Same thresholds for all filing statuses (no MFJ doubling). Source: DC OTR + Tax Foundation 2026.

roth.note: Graduated 4% to 10.75%; the 10.75% top only applies above $1M, while the 8.5% band covers roughly $60k-$250k where most conversion income lands (used here). IRA, 401(k), and Roth-conversion income is fully taxable; there is no retirement-income exclusion. Social Security is exempt.

taxContext.salesTaxNote: 6% (10% meals/alcohol); groceries, prescriptions exempt
